data.text: I am cautiously optimistic that FMOD might be able to do what we want. It's quite a different model than Wwise and I'm still getting my head around it but from what I can see, it has the concept of 'programmer sound" that can be incorporated into an effect chain.  One of the reasons to continue investigating this is to have a better way of managing all the sounds of the app with more flexible mixing groups.
Heavy currently exists unsupported on GitHub. https://github.com/enzienaudio/hvcc

card.name: Port sound engine to Wwise or FMOD.

data.text: I did the Wwise 101 certification course and it was amazing/fascinating. And I contacted Shawn Baptiste from AudioKinetic who I had met in Montreal. As great as Wwise is, he tells me that it does not do creating sound objects at runtime. There is a capability for referencing external files but even those need to be preprocessed by Wwise before being referenced. He recommended staying with Heavy. I'm not sure about that direction...there could be other more inclusive solutions. I have not looked at fmod yet.

card.name: Sometimes the word doesn't start playing when a client enters it.

data.text:
I found this in the archives: "ok the integration is done and is mostly working. But there is still an issue with the grain position not being set to the correct position right away when the pointer enters the word. From my tests it seems to be because the granular object does not take calls when the audio source is not playing. It may actually be a little more complicated than that. It seems like a timeout issue. So if I'm inactive for a while even a call to set metro to 1 won't work. So I had to stop using Metro. I just use Play() and Stop() to trigger the scrubbing. But then the issue remains...how do I set the grain before the play() starts? It's a slight annoyance but it's definitely there.

Also, hurray! no need for mixers. I am using the gvrAudioSource Gain parameter to vary the volume."

data.text:
Every object on the server is maintaining a list of the players that are currently pointing at it. When a player's pointer enters/leaves an object, he sends a signal to the server.

The first player in the list or the player who is currently recording sound on the object is called the sound owner. The server only accept CmdSetGranOffset from the sound owner.

data.text:
ok the integration is done and is mostly working. But there is still an issue with the grain position not being set to the correct position right away when the pointer enters the word. From my tests it seems to be because the granular object does not take calls when the audio source is not playing. It may actually be a little more complicated than that. It seems like a timeout issue. So if I'm inactive for a while even a call to set metro to 1 won't work. So I had to stop using Metro. I just use Play() and Stop() to trigger the scrubbing. But then the issue remains...how do I set the grain before the play() starts? It's a slight annoyance but it's definitely there.

Also, hurray! no need for mixers. I am using the gvrAudioSource Gain parameter to vary the volume.

data.text:
I drafted some of the details:

Every scene now has its own folder, holding the json file and audio files. New recordings will be saved in a temporary folder on the server. A new folder will be created when saving a scene, and the server will collect all the required audios into the folder. It means there will be duplicate audio files, but it is easy to organize.

Audios in the temporary folder will be deleted when you quit the server, not by the game clients/hosts.

data.text: I think this has something to do with the way credentials are acquired in the Watson SDK, on the phone. In the editor, and standalone, it does a straightforward read of the credentials from file. On the phone, it triggers a web request which is asynchronous. So the credentials are not marked as fetched the first time a recording is done.
